name: Mozzarella Cheese
recipeInstructions:
Mozzarella Cheese: This recipe is for a no-incubating, no-pressing, and no-curing Quick Mozzarella that freezes well, melts with the appropriate stringy, chewey texture, and is good used just for slicing and eating, too.
1. Heat 4 gallons of milk to 88 degrees F. (use a large stainless or enameled kettle)
2. Add 5 1/2 teaspoons citric acid dissolved in 1 cup cool water. (Citric acid can be found in the 'canning supply' section of the grocery; it is used to increase acidity in tomatoes)
3. Stir for 3 minutes.
4. Add 1 teaspoon liquid rennet diluted in 1/4 cup cool water. (Rennet is available in specialty shops and where wine making supplies are sold.) Stir in quickly as it sets in only 15 seconds.
5. Let set for 10 minutes, then cut curd into 1/2-inch cubes. (Cut from the top of the pan and then at an angle from 2 or 3 sides of the pan)
6. Let set for another 10 minutes.
7. Heat slowly to 105 degrees, stirring, then turn off the heat and stir slowly for 10 more minutes. (Heating to 105 degrees will take about 15 minutes)
8. Pour into a colander (can be lined with a layer of cheese cloth) and let drain for 5 minutes.
9. Remove to counter top and cut into 1-inch strips
10. Drop into one gallon of 170 degree water.
11. Stir, stretch, beat, and pummel for 10 minutes. Cheese will become smooth and silky.
12. Remove from water and knead as bread dough to remove any excess water and whey. Shape into a loaf.
13. Cover with very cold water until firm.
14. Soak in brine for 2 days. Make brine by dissolving 1/4 cup salt in 2 cups water. Make additional brine as needed to just cover cheese in a covered container and put into refrigerator.
15. After cheese has set in brine for 2 days, drain off the brine, dry, and wrap for storage in the refrigerator.
16. EAT! If you wish to make String Cheese of this type, when you get to step 12, after kneading, do not shape into loaf but put the cheese into another gallon of 170 degree water and stretch and fold the cheese. After folding and stretching several times, stretch the cheese out to about 3 feet in length and fold together once. Twist together tightly, fold and twist again, then fold and twist once more until you cannot twist it tighter. (Continue) This is the "braiding" that forms the strings. Soak in the brine for 2 - 3 days, then remove from brine, dry, and pull apart into large strings to serve or wrap tightly to store. A dairy thermometer is the most accurate for cheesemaking and floats to keep a constant check on the temperature. From Karen Visocky DDVD62B.
